title: Routing of Hippocampal Ripples to Subcortical Structures via the Lateral Septum
description: The mnemonic functions of hippocampal sharp wave ripples (SPW-Rs) have been studied extensively. Because hippocampal outputs affect not only cortical but also subcortical targets, we examined the impact of SPW-Rs on the firing patterns of lateral septal (LS) neurons in behaving rats. A large fraction of SPW-Rs were temporally locked to high-frequency oscillations (HFOs) (120–180 Hz) in LS, with strongest coupling during non-rapid eye movement (NREM) sleep, followed by waking immobility. However, coherence and spike-local field potential (LFP) coupling between the two structures were low, suggesting that HFOs are generated locally within the LS GABAergic population. This hypothesis was supported by optogenetic induction of HFOs in LS. Spiking of LS neurons was largely independent of the sequential order of spiking in SPW-Rs but instead correlated with the magnitude of excitatory synchrony of the hippocampal output. Thus, LS is strongly activated by SPW-Rs and may convey hippocampal population events to its hypothalamic and brainstem targets.

title: Disruption of theta-timescale spiking impairs learning but spares hippocampal replay
description: Rats transfected with a viral strategy to express a light-activated channel (ChR2) in PV+ neurons within the MS of PV-Cre transgenic rats. We also implanted an optic fiber targeting the MS as well as either silicon electrodes or multi-tetrode arrays targeting the hippocampus. Each of these animals received optical stimulation restricted to periods of locomotion on either a lineartrack, a W-shaped track, or both. The stimulation pattern was governed by a protocol that either activated MS PV neurons at a wide range of requested frequencies (“rhythmic stimulation”) or at specific phases of the ongoing theta rhythm (“phase-specific stimulation”) recorded in the hippocampus. Released in conjunction with the publication "Disruption of theta-timescale spiking impairs learning but spares hippocampal replay".

title: Mathematics and numerosity but not visuo-spatial working memory correlate with math-anxiety in adults
description:
Data in "Database" are organized in subfolders. Each subfolder contains data of the corresponding task.

Data from the Paper&Pencil tests are reported in the .xlsx file

 

Numerosity Estimation Task:

Each file contains a matrix called “matr”. Each row of the matrix “matr” is a trial. 

The columns contain the following information:

  • 1st: Subject response on numerosity 
  • 2nd: Test numerosity
  • 3rd: Subject response on numerosity 
  • 4th: 0 
  • 5th: Stimulus duration
  • 6th: Response time

 

Simple Calculation Task:

Each file contains a matrix called “MATR”. Each row of the matrix “MATR” is a trial. 

The columns contain the following information:

  • 1st: Number of trials 
  • 2nd: Subject response
  • 3rd: Response time 
  • 4th: First digit
  • 5th: mathematical symbols (x=1; +=2; –=3)
  • 6th: Second digit
  • 7th: Third digit
  • 8th: Forth digit

 

Complex Calculation Task:

Each file contains a matrix called “MATR”. Each row of the matrix “MATR” is a trial. 

The columns contain the following information:

  • 1st: Number of trials 
  • 2nd: Subject response
  • 3rd: Response time 
  • 4th: First digit
  • 5th: mathematical symbols (x=1; +=2; –=3; /=4)
  • 6th: Second digit

 

VSWM Task:

Data from forward and backward conditions are located in two different subfolders.

Each file contains a matrix called “Span”, with the corresponding VSWM span.

 

For more details, please contact the first author.

title: Robust neuronal dynamics in premotor cortex during motor planning
description: Neural activity in the premotor cortex maintains representations that bridge past and future events, such as motor planning, often over several seconds. This study uses electrophysiology and optogenetic perturbations in the mouse premotor cortex (specifically the anterior lateral motor cortex, ALM) to probe the robustness of these persistent neural representations. The research demonstrates that preparatory activity is remarkably robust to large-scale unilateral silencing, with detailed neural dynamics being quickly and selectively restored by the network. However, this selectivity does not recover after bilateral silencing, suggesting that perturbations in one hemisphere are corrected by information from the other. This redundancy across selectively coupled modules is a hallmark of robust control systems. The dataset includes silicon probe recordings and optogenetic perturbation data (using VGAT-ChR2-EYFP and PV-ires-cre mice) during an object location discrimination task.

title: Developing mouse whole-brain transcriptomic cell type atlas - MERSCOPE v1
description: Spatial transcriptomics atlas of the entire brain of a developing mouse (P4). We profiled 500 genes which were selected to maximize our ability to relate each cell to one of the 5200 cell types defined in our RNAseq-based taxonomy. We image 14 µm section which were acquired at an interval of 100 µm. Individual cell were identified using cell segmentation algorithms to a total of ~9.2 million cells.  The MERFISH datasets not only provide the possibility for accurate spatial annotation of major cell types at subclass level, but also reveal very fine resolution spatial distinctions or gradients for cell types within each subclass. Dataset was resegmented using cellpose

title: Data for Cell-type-specific inhibitory circuitry from a connectomic census of mouse visual cortex

Data for the paper: Cell-type-specific inhibitory circuitry from a connectomic census of mouse visual cortex, Nature 640, 2025

In brief, this data archive includes information about the skeleton morphology and synaptic features of neurons whose cell bodies fell within a 100 micron by 100 micron column spanning all layers of mouse visual cortex. See MICrONs-Explorer for a full description of the broader volume and how it was collected.

The data here include both data tables of cell locations, neuronal features, synapse lists, and more, as well as files containing morphological descriptions of all neurons used for the analysis in the initial version of the preprint. See the README.md file for more complete information about the individual files.

Note: Data has been updated with post-publication files.

description: This dataset is part of a series of spatial transcriptomics experiments targetting specific cell types and regions of the brain that have shown gene expression changes with age in the mouse based on scRNA seq data. Four broad regions of the brain (cortex, striatum, midbrian, and hindbrain) were profiled across young adult (2 month) and aged (18 month) male and female C57BL6/J mice using Resolve Bioscience's Molecular Cartography spatial transcriptomics platform. This experiment includes in situ gene expression profiling for 100 genes. The 100 gene panel was designed to target non-neuronal cell types in the brain, and includes candidate DE genes that change with age across these cell types. This dataset is reprocessed data related to https://doi.brainimagelibrary.org/doi/10.35077/g.1157

title: Transcriptomic correlates of neuron electrophysiological diversity
description: How neuronal diversity emerges from complex patterns of gene expression remains poorly understood. Here we present an approach to understand electrophysiological diversity through gene expression by integrating pooled- and single-cell transcriptomics with intracellular electrophysiology. Using neuroinformatics methods, we compiled a brain-wide dataset of 34 neuron types with paired gene expression and intrinsic electrophysiological features from publically accessible sources, the largest such collection to date. We identified 420 genes whose expression levels significantly correlated with variability in one or more of 11 physiological parameters. We next trained statistical models to infer cellular features from multivariate gene expression. Such models were predictive of gene-electrophysiological relationships in an independent collection of 12 visual cortex cell types from the Allen Institute, suggesting that these correlations might reflect general principles relating expression patterns to phenotypic diversity across very different cell types. Many associations reported here have the potential to provide new insights into how neurons generate functional diversity, and correlations of ion channel genes like Gabrd and Scn1a (Nav1.1) with resting potential and spiking frequency are consistent with known causal mechanisms. Our work highlights the promise and inherent challenges in using cell type-specific transcriptomics to understand the mechanistic origins of neuronal diversity.

title: Cortical cell assemblies and their underlying connectivity: an in silico study
description:
Dataset linked to the article with the same title

  • simulation_config.zip: contains SONATA config files needed to re-run an exemplary simulation (after downloading the circuit from 10.5281/zenodo.7930275). In order to run it, paths in circuit_config.json, and simulation_config.json have to be updated!
  • assemblies.h5 is a dataset produced (and can be easily opened) by: assemblyfire (see GitHub README for more documentation) and serves as a basis for the manuscript. As the assemblies are the results of an unsupervised clustering (of high activity time bins) the resulting labels are not necessary meaningful. In the manuscript we have ordered the assemblies (from early to late responding ones, and from pattern A to J responsive ones) but the HDF5 file still stores the original labels. The mapping from the "random" labels to the ones presented in our article is stored in the config files on GitHub.

The development of this dataset was supported by funding to the Blue Brain Project, a research center of the École polytechnique fédérale de Lausanne (EPFL), from the Swiss government’s ETH Board of the Swiss Federal Institutes of Technology.

title: Mapping track density changes in nigrostriatal and extranigral pathways in Parkinson's disease
description: Here, we applied a novel diffusion-weighted imaging approach-track density imaging (TDI). Twenty-seven non-demented Parkinson's patients (mean disease duration: 5years, mean score on the Hoehn & Yahr scale=1.5) were compared with 26 elderly controls matched for age, sex, and education level. Track density images were created by sampling each subject's spatially normalized fiber tracks in 1mm isotropic intervals and counting the fibers that passed through each voxel. Whole-brain voxel-based analysis was performed and significance was assessed with permutation testing. Statistically significant increases in track density were found in the Parkinson's patients, relative to controls. Clusters were distributed in disease-relevant areas including motor, cognitive, and limbic networks. From the lower medulla to the diencephalon and striatum, clusters encompassed the known location of the locus coeruleus and pedunculopontine nucleus in the pons, and from the substantia nigra up to medial aspects of the posterior putamen, bilaterally. The results identified in brainstem and nigrostriatal pathways show a large overlap with the known distribution of neuropathological changes in non-demented PD patients. Our results also support an early involvement of limbic and cognitive networks in Parkinson's disease.

title: Functional MRI mapping of dynamic visual features during natural viewing in the macaque
description:
The included data consists of a set of r-square maps computed from a stepwise regress. The macaques viewed 15 five minute movies repeatedly while fMRI responses were recorded.  Six visual features were coded from each movie, 3 high-level (Faces, Animals, Extremities) and 3 low-level (Luminance, Contrast, Motion), which were then submitted to a stepwise regression at each voxel in the volume.  For each voxel a total variance explained was calculated along with the individual variance explained by each of the six feature regressors.  
In a separate set of sessions, the subjects participated in classic block paradigm contrasting macaque faces and phase scrambled versions of the faces.  We calculated the T-maps of the contrast between the faces and scrambled images, with the goal of mapping the locations of the face processing system, commonly known as the face patches.

title: Source and Tissue Agnostic Reconstruction of Neurons
description:
Recent successes in deep learning have started to impact neuroscience. Of particular significance are claims that current segmentation algorithms achieve "super-human" accuracy in an area known as connectomics. However, as we will show, these algorithms do not effectively generalize beyond the particular source and brain tissues used for training -- severely limiting their usability by the broader neuroscience community. To fill this gap, we describe a novel connectomics challenge for source- and tissue-agnostic reconstruction of neurons (STARN), which favors broad generalization over fitting specific datasets. We first demonstrate that current state-of-the-art approaches to neuron segmentation perform poorly on the challenge. We further describe a novel convolutional recurrent neural network module that combines short-range horizontal connections within a processing stage and long-range top-down connections between stages. The resulting architecture establishes the state of the art on the STARN challenge -- improving the prospect for computer vision to allow for widespread fully-automated connectomics analysis.

Here, we address the poor generalization of computer vision systems in connectomics with a novel challenge: the source- and tissue-agnostic reconstruction of neurons. This challenge presents a "training" dataset consisting of five publicly available and annotated tissue volumes representing a variety of organisms and imaging configurations (CREMI, FIB-25, and SNEMI3D); evaluation is performed on an independent volume [1] annotated by our group. We will demonstrate that the STARN challenge defeats state-of-the-art systems for neuron reconstruction.

title: Automated Anatomical Labeling of Activations in SPM Using a Macroscopic Anatomical Parcellation of the MNI MRI Single-Subject Brain
Mapping the Neural Dynamics of Locomotion across the Drosophila Brain
description: Walking is a fundamental mode of locomotion, yet its neural correlates are unknown at brain-wide scale in any animal. We use volumetric two-photon imaging to map neural activity associated with walking across the entire brain of Drosophila. We detect locomotor signals in approximately 40% of the brain, identify a global signal associated with the transition from rest to walking, and define clustered neural signals selectively associated with changes in forward or angular velocity. These networks span functionally diverse brain regions, and include regions that have not been previously linked to locomotion. We also identify time-varying trajectories of neural activity that anticipate future movements, and that represent sequential engagement of clusters of neurons with different behavioral selectivity. These motor maps suggest a dynamical systems framework for constructing walking maneuvers reminiscent of models of forelimb reaching in primates and set a foundation for understanding how local circuits interact across large-scale networks.

title: Going Around Phase Defects: Reliable Phase Mapping for Realistic Data (Videos)
description:
This data repository contains supplementary videos referenced in the linked paper. Each video provides dynamic visualizations used to support figures in the manuscript.

Included files:

  • carto_posterior.mp4
    Video showing a wavefront traveling across the left atrium during atrial tachycardia (posterior view), supplementary to Figure 3.5.
    The local activation time map, phase field, and naive and extended phase mapping analysis are shown.

  • carto_anterior.mp4
    Video showing a wavefront traveling across the left atrium during atrial tachycardia (anterior view), supplementary to Figure 3.5.
    The local activation time map, phase field, and naive and extended phase mapping analysis are shown.

  • prepacing.mp4
    Video of action potential during the prepacing to the cardiac tissue. This procedure is done for all simulations.

  • linear_core.mp4
    Video showing a wavefront traveling across a 2D simulation of a linear rotor core, supplementary to Figure 3.1.
    The action potential, phase field, and naive and extended phase mapping analysis are shown.

  • conduction_block.mp4
    Video showing a wavefront traveling across a 2D simulation of parallel activation around a functional conduction block., supplementary to Figure 3.2.
    The action potential, phase field, and naive and extended phase mapping analysis are shown.

  • near_complete.mp4
    Video showing a wavefront traveling across a 2D simulation complete and near-complete rotation around scar tissue, supplementary to Figure 3.3.
    The action potential, phase field, and naive and extended phase mapping analysis are shown.

  • optical.mp4
    Video showing a wavefront traveling across of the left ventricle of a rat heart during  ventricular fibrillation, supplementary to Figure 3.4.
    The optical map, phase field, and naive and extended phasemapping analysis are shown.

title: Pre-Operative Mapping of Language Regions Using rsFMRI in Gliomas: A Feasibility Study
description:
Pre-Operative Mapping of Language Regions Using rsfMRI in Gliomas: A Feasibility Study

Introduction:
The pre-operative mapping of language regions in glioma patients is crucial for preserving functional integrity during surgical resection. Traditional intraoperative mapping via direct cortical stimulation requires patient cooperation, which can prolong surgery and introduce variability. This study explores the feasibility of using resting-state functional MRI (rsfMRI) as an alternative for pre-operative language mapping, allowing for more accurate and efficient neurosurgical planning.

Methods:
The study employs rsfMRI to analyze the resting-state connectivity of language regions, particularly Broca’s and Wernicke’s areas. Functional networks are mapped using Independent Component Analysis (ICA) and aligned to a functional atlas via nonlinear registration (‘syn’ transformation). Thresholding and Z-score normalization are applied to refine functional segmentation. Additionally, volumetric loss in language regions is quantitatively analyzed to correlate with functional impairment, assessed via the Addenbrooke’s Cognitive Examination III (ACE-III).

Key Findings:

  • Volume loss in language regions correlates with cognitive decline. A case of Broca’s area volume loss (10,457 mm³) was associated with a fluency score of 8/14, while Wernicke’s area showed no volume loss and had a comprehension score of 24/26.
  • Severe functional displacement in glioma patients. The patient with the largest midline shift (11 mm) also exhibited the greatest functional shift (Hausdorff distance = 24 mm, Dice = 0.08).
  • Functional changes are non-linear. More than 70% volume loss is needed before functional impairment becomes apparent, suggesting a threshold effect.
  • Resting-state fMRI can detect functional reorganization. One patient with better bilateral language representation showed less correlation between volume loss and functional decline, indicating neuroplasticity.

Clinical Relevance:
Using rsfMRI for language mapping offers several advantages over traditional intraoperative stimulation:

  1. Patient-independent mapping: Eliminates the need for awake cooperation during surgery.
  2. Pre-operative functional planning: Allows for better localization of eloquent cortex regions, reducing post-operative deficits.
  3. Neuro-navigation integration: Functional shifts due to tumor mass effect can be accounted for, enhancing surgical precision.

Conclusion:
This feasibility study demonstrates the potential of rsfMRI in glioma surgery planning. While further validation with larger datasets is required, the approach offers a non-invasive, patient-friendly, and efficient method for mapping language networks. Future studies may refine this methodology to improve functional preservation and post-surgical outcomes in neurosurgical oncology.

title: Going Around Phase Defects: Reliable Phase Mapping for Realistic Data (Optical Mapping Data)
description:
This repository contains optical mapping data of ventricular fibrillation (VF) acquired from an isolated rat heart. The dataset was analyzed and presented in the paper:

Going Around Phase Defects: Reliable Phase Mapping for Realistic Data.

The data correspond to a single experimental case with maximally increased gap junction coupling achieved by administration of 80 nM rotigaptide. This condition was chosen to test the proposed phase-mapping methods under stable rotor dynamics.

A Sprague–Dawley rat (250–300 g, 9–12 weeks old) heart was explanted and perfused ex vivo using a Langendorff setup with Krebs–Henseleit buffer maintained at 37 ± 0.5 °C and pH 7.35 ± 0.05. After 15 minutes of stabilization, the voltage-sensitive dye RH237 (40 µL, 5 mg/mL in DMSO) and the excitation–contraction uncoupler blebbistatin (30 µM loading, then 10 µM maintenance) were added. Gap junction coupling was enhanced by 80 nM rotigaptide. Ventricular fibrillation was induced electrically at the base of the left ventricle (MicroPace, USA) and sustained using pinacidil (30 µM).

Optical mapping was performed using RH237 excitation at 530 nm. Emitted fluorescence was recorded with high-resolution CMOS cameras at a sampling rate of 1 kHz, with a spatial resolution of 128 × 80 pixels and a pixel size of approximately 100–125 µm.

Data preprocessing was performed in MATLAB and included background subtraction, 3 × 3 spatial binning, low-pass filtering using a 50th-order FIR filter with a cutoff frequency of 100 Hz, and drift removal using a 100 ms sliding window. Additional details on the signal processing pipeline are described in Laughner et al. (2012)

title: An automated labeling system for subdividing the human cerebral cortex on MRI scans into gyral based regions of interest
10x Genomics single-cell RNA-seq of colorectal cancer patient tumor and peripheral blood specimens enriched for hybrid cells
description: This study profiles tumor-macrophage hybrid cells in colorectal cancer using single-cell transcriptomic and epigenomic approaches. Fusion-derived hybrid cells were generated by co-culture of MC38-H2B-RFP colorectal cancer cells with beta-actin-GFP bone marrow-derived macrophages, and patient-derived hybrid cells were isolated from primary colorectal tumors and peripheral blood by fluorescence-activated cell sorting based on co-expression of epithelial (EpCAM and/or ECAD) and immune (CD45) markers. The study compares hybrid cells with parental tumor and immune controls and includes artificial macrophage-tumor doublets generated on the ICELL8 platform to distinguish bona fide hybrids from technical doublets. This submission contains 10x Genomics single-cell RNA-seq libraries from de-identified colorectal cancer patient tumor and peripheral blood specimens in which pooled cells were enriched for hybrid cells to capture the surrounding cellular landscape. A subset of colorectal cancer patient primary tumor and peripheral blood specimens (n=3) with sufficient hybrid cell yield was profiled by 10x Genomics single-cell RNA-seq (in supplement to plate-based scRNA using SMART seq of isolated hybrid cells). Pooled non-hybrid primary tumor or peripheral blood cells were spiked with an enrichment of EpCAM and/or ECAD positive and CD45 positive hybrid cells. Nuclei were isolated and processed using the 10x Genomics Single Cell 3' Gene Expression kit.

description: Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) is used to establish a macroscopic reference coordinate system of laminar and cytoarchitectural boundaries. title: Reported access resistance values from in vivo whole-cell patch recordings of neurons in mouse, rat and cat cortex, compared with values from the present study.
Visualization and characterization of membrane lesions
description: In this study, real-time imaging was used to visualize individual electropores and demonstrate their persistence in electroporated cells. HEK 923 cells were cultured on Indium Tin Oxide (ITO)-coated coverslips and loaded with the Cal520 fluorescent indicator for visualization. Real-time imaging was performed in Total Internal Reflection Fluorescent (TIRF) mode. Electrophysiological manipulations were applied using a patch clamp in voltage clamp mode, set in a whole-cell configuration. The protocol included a command voltage of 0 mV interrupted by a -50 mV pulse for 50 ms and a -400 mV pulse for 1 ms at frame 91, returning to -50 mV for 50 ms. Additionally, 0.4 seconds into the second and third stacks, the command voltage was changed from 0 mV to -50 mV for 50 ms. The study was supported in part by NIH 15R21EY034803 and NIH 1R21EY034258.

title: Four-Chamber Human Heart Model for the Simulation of Cardiac Electrophysiology and Cardiac Mechanics
description:
Changes in version 1.1 compared to version 1.0:

  • Ventricular fiber orientation changed to 66° on the endocardial and −41° on the epicardial surface
  • Electrophysiology mesh was resampled
  • Updated material tags in EP mesh
  • Further details can be found in the CardioMechanics GitHub repository new reference paper:

Gerach, T.; Loewe, A. Differential effects of mechano-electric feedback mechanisms on whole-heart activation, repolarization, and tension. The Journal of Physiology 2024. https://doi.org/10.1113/JP285022 

This repository contains a four-chamber model of the human heart which is ready to use for simulations of cardiac electrophysiology and cardiac mechanics problems. When using this dataset, please also cite the accompanying paper

Gerach, T.; Schuler, S.; Fröhlich, J.; Lindner, L.; Kovacheva, E.; Moss, R.; Wülfers, E.M.; Seemann, G.; Wieners, C.; Loewe, A. Electro-Mechanical Whole-Heart Digital Twins: A Fully Coupled Multi-Physics Approach. Mathematics 2021, 9, 1247. https://doi.org/10.3390/math9111247

The cardiac anatomy was manually segmented from magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) data of a 33 year old male volunteer. The volunteer provided informed consent and the study was approved by the IRB of Heidelberg University Hospital (Fritz et al., 2014).
The MRI data were acquired using a 1.5 T MR tomography system and consist of a static whole heart image stack taken during diastasis as well as time-resolved images in several long and short axis slices. Based on the segmentation, we first labeled the atria and the ventricles. The geometry was extended by a representation of the mitral valve, the tricuspid valve, the aortic valve and the pulmonary valve. Additionally, we closed the endo- and epicardial surfaces of the atria and added truncated pulmonary veins, vena cavae as well as the ascending aorta and pulmonary artery. Furthermore, we added a concentric layer of tissue around the entire heart which phenomenologically represents the influence of the pericardium and the surrounding tissue.

Two tetrahedral meshes were created using Gmsh (Geuzaine et al., 2009): (1) the mechanical reference domain (M.vtu) with 128,976 elements (on average 3.17 mm edge length) and (2) the electrophysiological reference domain (EP.vtu) as a subset of M with 50,058,295 elements (on average 0.4 mm edge length).
We used rule-based methods to assign the myofiber orientation on EP: Wachter et al. (2015) was used for the atria and Bayer et al. (2012) for the ventricles. The fiber angle in the ventricles was chosen as +60° and -60° on the endocardial and epicardial surface, respectively. The sheet angle was set to -65° on the endocardium and 25° on the epicardium. Github repositories to these fiber generation tools are given in the sidebar. All geometry files are given in millimeter (mm).

  • Data:
    We provide time resolved data evaluated from cine MRI data, which can be used for model calibration.
    • Wall thickening (17AHA_WT.txt) / fractional wall thickening (17AHA_fractionalWT.txt) in the 17 AHA segments of the left ventricle
    • Atrioventricular plane displacement (AVPD.txt) and velocity (AVPV.txt) as well as the displacement of all tracked points used for AVPD calculation (AVPD_trackedPoints.txt)
    • Left and right ventricular volume (Volume_LV_RV.txt). RV volume is only available for end-diastole and end systole.
  • Surfaces:
    This directory contains *.stl files with triangulated surfaces on which boundary conditions can be applied.
    • cavityXX.stl: blood volume of the LV, RV, LA, RA
    • epicard.stl: the whole epicardium
    • outerPeri.stl and outerTrunks.stl: surfaces for Dirichlet boundary conditions
    • master.stl and slave.stl: surfaces used for the frictionless contact problem described in Fritz et al. (2014)
  • TetGen:
    Contains the geometry M.vtu in the TetGen file format. T4 mesh with 4 node tetrahedrons and 3 node triangles.
    • .bases: fiber, sheet, and normal orientation at quadrature points of all elements
    • .node: vertex coordinates
    • .ele: list of tetrahedra
    • .sur: list of triangles
  • EP.vtu:
    Contains the cell arrays Fiber and Material.
  • M.vtu:
    Contains the cell arrays Fiber, Sheet, Sheetnormal, Material, and Label.
  • LabelIDs.txt:
    List of Label and Material identification numbers and corresponding anatomical structures.

title: Grosmark: Sleep-dependent synaptic renormalization in hippocampus
description: Silicon probe recordings from hippocampal CA1 (Achilles, Buddy, Cicero, Gatsby) during wake and sleep. Synaptic downscaling across sleep.

title: Replication Data for: Controlling morpho-electrophysiological variability of neurons with detailed biophysical models
description: Replication Data for: Controlling morpho-electrophysiological variability of neurons with detailed biophysical models to use with example/paper_figures scripts in https://github.com/BlueBrain/emodel-generalisation

title: Sequential Inference VBM
Using Python and Julia to Model the Gating of Ion Channel Proteins
description:
This poster was presented at PyData Global 2020 and demonstrates a three-step computational workflow for modelling ion channel gating kinetics using Julia and Python. The biological system used is the pacemaker HCN (Hyperpolarization-activated, Cyclic Nucleotide-gated) channel, which regulates rhythmic activity in the heart and brain.


The workflow consists of: (1) numerical simulation of a four-state allosteric gating model (CR ↔ CA, OR ↔ OA) using DifferentialEquations.jl; (2) global optimization of model parameters against patch-clamp electrophysiology data using BlackBoxOptim.jl and GCMAES.jl with simultaneous calibration across multiple datasets; and (3) Markov chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling with PyMC3 to approximate the posterior distribution of model parameters and quantify uncertainty. Model identifiability is assessed by evaluating predictive performance on held-out validation datasets.


The poster is intended for a data science audience and emphasizes the practical use of the Julia/Python scientific ecosystem for biophysical modelling. Electrophysiology data shown are unpublished; access to underlying data is available upon reasonable request.

title: Stark: Hippocampal interneuron control of sharp-wave ripples
description: Silicon probe recordings from hippocampal CA1 with optogenetic interneuron control. Mechanisms of ripple generation and termination.

title: Neuronal culture growth simulation dataset
description:
Data from four simulations of growth and development of a culture of initially dissociated neurons, extracted from raw HDF5 files produced by simulation (not uploaded, as they would be 120GB total). See:

Kawasaki, F.and M. Stiber, “A simple model of cortical culture growth: burst property dependence on network composition and activity”, Biological Cybernetics 108(4): 423–443, doi:10.1007/s00422-014-0611-9, 2014.

for basic information. Dataset generated and extracted for subsequent use and publication by the University of Washington Bothell Intelligent Networks lab.

title: Brain atlas data for the BBP Somatosensory Cortex model
description: This dataset contains the brain atlas used for the construction of the BBP Somatosensory cortex model. The model itself is described in the following manuscripts: Modeling and Simulation of Neocortical Micro- and Mesocircuitry. Part I: Anatomy and Modeling and Simulation of Neocortical Micro- and Mesocircuitry. Part II: Physiology and Experimentation The dataset describes the geometry of the brain region modeled and the target densities of the various neuron types contained in the region. To use the dataset, first uncompress using a tool such as unzip. To use the contained files see or recommended tools in the notes below. Files: [cell_density]*.nrrd: Describes the voxelized cell densities of various neuron types. brain_regions.nrrd: Describes the voxelized locations of brain regions. That is, each voxel is annotated with a region identifier. hierarchy.json: A lookup from region identifier (used in brain_regions.nrrd) to region name. Also describes the hierarchy of brain regions. flatmap.nrrd: Describes the locations of each voxel in a flattened coordinate system. Coordinates are flattened orthogonal to layer boundaries. These coordinates are useful for visualization and analysis. For details see our related publication . orientation.nrrd: Describes the orientation towards the cortical surface at the center of each voxel. More info in the same publication as above. height.nrrd: Describes the total height of the cortex at the location given by the center of the voxel.

title: Reliable individual-level neural markers of high-level language processing: A necessary precursor for relating neural variability to behavioral and genetic variability
Oscillatory Dynamics Supporting Semantic Cognition: MEG Evidence for the Contribution of the Anterior Temporal Lobe Hub and Modality-Specific Spokes
Intentionally vs. Spontaneously Prolonged Gaze: A MEG Study of Active Gaze-Based Interaction
description: This dataset accompanies the published paper "Intentionally vs. Spontaneously Prolonged Gaze: A MEG Study of Active Gaze‑Based Interaction" (Cortex, 189:76–96, 10.1016/j.cortex.2025.05.010; Vasilyev et al., 2025), which explores neural mechanisms underlying intentional and spontaneous gaze behavior during gaze‑based human–computer interaction. Magnetoencephalography (MEG) and eye‑tracking data were collected from participants engaged in a specially designed gaze‑controlled computer game, where actions required intentional fixation of visual targets. Sample Size Participants: 29 healthy individuals (mean age: 25 years; 12 females). Trials: 6,748 total trials, with each participant contributing over 100 trials per condition. The dataset is balanced, containing an equal number of trials for the Intentional and Spontaneous conditions. Recording Setup MEG: 306-channel Elekta Neuromag TRIUX™ system. Eye-tracking: EyeLink 1000 Plus (monocular recording, 1000 Hz). Stimuli and Data Labeling Participants played the gaze-controlled game "EyeLines". Each move required selecting an object (a colored ball) by maintaining gaze fixation on it for at least 500 ms, after which a visual "selected" feedback animation appeared. To complete the selection, participants had to subsequently perform a confirmation dwell on a designated visual "button". Gaze dwells on balls occurred in two distinct contexts: Intentional dwell: Participants purposefully fixated on a ball intending to select it. This dwell was followed by a saccade toward the confirmation button approximately 700–800 ms after initial fixation onset. This interval includes: Initial dwell: 500 ms Feedback appearance: approximately 60–100 ms Feedback processing and saccadic response initiation: approximately 100–150 ms Spontaneous dwell: Participants fixated a ball spontaneously during gameplay, typically while deliberating potential moves, analyzing ball positions, or visually exploring the game board without explicit intention to select. These spontaneous dwells also triggered the "selected" animation but were not followed by confirmatory button dwell. Epoch Window MEG data epochs represent signals related to each dwell, spanning from −2000 ms to +1999 ms relative to dwell-initiating fixation onset. Due to the game design, controlled comparisons between Intentional and Spontaneous epochs are valid only within the 0 to 500 ms interval, as behavioral activity following feedback varies significantly between conditions. Data Preprocessing MEG data underwent initial post-acquisition preprocessing using the MaxFilter routine, including: Temporal signal space separation (tSSS) with 10 s buffer and correlation threshold of 0.9 Movement correction (MaxMove) Head-origin normalization This preprocessing is part of post-acquisition handling and represents the raw data state for this dataset. Epoch synchronization between MEG and eye-tracking data was ensured by matching synchronization triggers generated by the eye-tracker server with MEG triggers.

title: The role of language in deductive reasoning: evidence from global aphasia
description: The human species is marked by sophisticated cognitive capacities and by the possession of language. Within the philosophical and cognitive sciences there is a debate regarding the role of language in human reasoning. One claim states that grammar (combinatorial rules) is necessary for various forms of reasoning; as a result, significant language impairment is expected to disrupt logical reasoning. This project will provide new evidence to the debate by exploring performance in a unique sample of people – those with severe/global aphasia (language impairment following brain-injury), and examining whether a human mind, with little access to language resources, can apply deductive reasoning rules. To this end, we will be using complex Sudoku grids, which require the application of deduction rules (e.g. modus ponens). To address potential criticism that retention of these abilities is a result of functional reorganisation, we will utilise fMRI, comparing brain activations of aphasic and neurotypical participants.

title: Oscillatory Multi-Timescale Mechanisms Underlying Audiovisual Sequence Prediction Imaging Neuroscience (dataset)
description: This dataset supports the article "Oscillatory Multi-Timescale Mechanisms Underlying Audiovisual Sequence Prediction" published in Imaging Neuroscience. It contains MEG data recorded from human participants performing an audiovisual serial prediction task. In this task, participants learned stimulus sequences and monitored whether subsequent probe items followed or violated the expected sequence. Analysis demonstrated both task-related changes in oscillatory power and coupling, either within or cross frequencies. And behavior performance varied as a function of the phase delay of the coupling. This dataset provides valuable insight into the multi-timescale neural dynamics underlying multisensory sequence prediction.

title: Temporal expectation and attention jointly modulate auditory oscillatory activity in the beta band
description: This is an MEG dataset where we manipulated temporal expectation and temporal attention to pure tones. We analyzed the oscillatory signatures of these tones in sensors overlaying auditory cortex. A full description of the study setup is described in the file named 'Methods'.

title: Cochlear activity in silent cue-target intervals shows a theta-rhythmic pattern and is correlated to attentional alpha and theta modulations
description: Preprocessed data and analysis-scripts needed to reproduce the figures and statistics of the paper "Cochlear activity in silent cue-target intervals shows a theta-rhythmic pattern and is correlated to attentional alpha and theta modulations".

title: Volume-wise analysis of fMRI time series allows for accurate prediction of language lateralization
description: Group results maps from our language-lateralization manuscript. Full analysis code available on https://github.com/mwegrzyn/volume-wise-language

title: Data_Sheet_2_Intracranial recordings reveal high-frequency activity in the human temporal-parietal cortex supporting non-literal language processing.PDF
2021-hfMEG
description: Noninvasive neuromagnetic single-trial analysis of human neocortical population spikes; MEG data and scripts

title: Oscillatory episodes of brain activity derived from the OMEGA dataset
description: This dataset contains source-reconstructed magnetoencephalography (MEG) brain activity (1,925 voxels) from a sample of 128 healthy participants from The Open MEG Archive (OMEGA, version 2.0; Niso et al., 2016). For each participant, episodes of oscillatory brain activity were identified using sBOSC (Stern et al., 2025; https://github.com/necog-UAM/sBOSC.-Oscillation-Detection-Algorithm-), an extension of the BOSC (Better OSCillation Detection) family of algorithms. As in previous BOSC-based approaches, sBOSC detects oscillatory episodes that exceed both a predefined power threshold and a minimum duration criterion. In addition, sBOSC incorporates spatial information into the detection procedure by identifying local maxima across the reconstructed brain volume. Oscillatory episodes are further constrained to frequencies corresponding to peaks in the power spectra, ensuring that the detected events reflect genuine oscillatory activity. Detailed information about the procedure used by sBOSC to extract oscillatory episodes can be found in Stern et al. (2025). Additional information on how oscillatory episodes can be used to derive relevant measures of brain oscillatory activity (e.g., episode duration or mean episode power) can be found in Stern & Capilla (2026).

title: Interstimulus-interval dependence in auditory cortex reflects perceptual organization of streams under informational masking [data]
description: The perceptual saliency of a regular sound sequence embedded into a well-chosen multi-tone-masker background fluctuates despite the absence of change in the physical stimulus. When such tone streams are perceived, each tone of the stream evokes a negative-going response referred to as awareness-related negativity (ARN). Here we probe if the ARN amplitude shows a dependence on the inter-stimulus interval (ISI) within perceived tone streams, similar to the N1 response evoked by unmasked tone streams independently of a task. Isochronous target streams were presented under multi-tone masking with ISIs of 500, 850, and 1200 ms, while listeners indicated when they perceived the onset of the target streams. A control experiment applied the same tone streams without masking in the presence of a second, narrow-band noise stream. In two separate runs, listeners either attended the tone streams and indicated their beginning and ending, or they attended the distractor streams, in which they performed a deviance detection task. Activity in auditory cortex was recorded with magnetoencephalography. Experiment 1 revealed a significant amplitude increment of the ARN with increasing ISI. In Experiment 2, the known amplitude dependence of the N1 was observed for attended and unattended runs alike. Critically, the negative difference waveform (Nd) between attended and unattended runs did not increase with the ISI. These results support an interpretation of the ARN as being related to the neural formation of an auditory stream and not as a pure reflection of attentional response enhancement.

title: Neural complexity as a marker of pain perception
description:
1. Background

When receiving a sensory stimulus, the brain possesses the capacity to balance information processing between chaos and redundancy, amplification and quenching, differentiation and integration(Kurth et al., 2010; Liu et al., 2025; O’Byrne & Jerbi, 2022; Sun et al., 2026). In pain perception, the brain differentiates nociceptive inputs with distinct physical characteristics (e.g., temperature, intensity) from other sensory events and integrates these nociceptive inputs within large-scale networks that assign salience and contextual meaning(Borsook et al., 2013; Kastrati et al., 2022; Menon & Uddin, 2010; Pak & Hashmi, 2023). Several theoretical frameworks have sought to characterize the “complexity” with which the brain processes information. While classification may vary according to different theoretical frameworks of complexity, generally one set of approaches examines the balance between stability and instability in response to a perturbation, while another quantifies signal propagation patterns through space and the emergence of unique system configurations (O’Byrne & Jerbi, 2022). Within the first framework, the Perturbational Complexity Index (PCI) measures the spatiotemporal richness of the brain’s response to stimulation (Breyton et al., 2025; Casali et al., 2013; Comolatti et al., 2019). Within the second, grounded in avalanches criticality theory, the functional repertoire indexes the diversity of unique functional patterns exhibited by a network over time (Breyton et al., 2025). In this study, we investigate how painful and non-painful thermal experiences relate to neural complexity, estimated using these two complementary measures.

Although previous studies primarily examined PCI using TMS-evoked responses (Casali et al., 2013; Casarotto et al., 2016; Sinitsyn et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2022), we recently demonstrated that peripheral thermosensory stimulation also modulates this index (Montemagno et al., 2026). Warm, non-painful stimuli were associated with lower complexity, while more intense and painful heat or non-painful cold stimuli produced higher values, suggesting that at least temperature changes can be indexed by PCI. 
Despite heterogeneous results in previous EEG studies on pain, several spectral patterns have been reported in response to transient and sustained pain: increases in delta and gamma power, decreases in alpha and beta power, and increases in functional connectivity and delta coherence (Bott et al., 2025; Zis et al., 2022), hinting at a network reorganization that can be measured by complexity metrics.
These findings both suggest that PCI is sensitive to differences in sensory features and raises the question of its ability to distinguish painful and non-painful conditions. However, since our previous study conflated perceptual features (e.g. intensity, painfulness) and physical stimulus characteristics (e.g. temperature), their respective contributions to perturbational complexity remain unclear.

To address this limitation, we developed an experimental paradigm in which identical physical stimuli yield different perceptual outcomes, enabling us to examine neural complexity across the boundary between non-painful and painful experiences. To achieve this aim, we will make use of stimulations delivered at perceptual thresholds. Our primary model will be based on the heat pain threshold (HPT): thermal stimuli will be calibrated such that the same temperature is perceived as painful on roughly half of the trials (i.e., stimulation at the HPT) (Prins, 2013). A parallel approach will be employed for the thermal grill illusion (TGI), in which warm and cold inputs are spatially interlaced to elicit an illusion of pain (Fardo et al., 2020). Using individual calibration, we will identify warm–cold combinations that produce an illusory pain percept on approximately 50% of trials, while each component (cold or warm alone) remains non-painful (TGI threshold, from now on TGI). As control conditions, we will also present unimodal stimuli (innocuous warm, innocuous cold, and suprathreshold heat pain) that are physically identical (innocuous stimuli) or minimally different (suprathreshold heat pain) to the threshold temperatures used in TGI and HPT conditions. Together, these manipulations provide a rigorous framework for dissociating the contributions of perceptual categorization and physical stimulus properties to neural complexity.	


2. Hypotheses

In this study, we aim to quantify the complexity of sensory-evoked brain responses with two different measurements, PCI and functional repertoire. Given our previous work in which we already investigated the sensitivity of PCI to the features of thermal stimuli (Montemagno et al., 2026), our primary outcome will be based on PCI calculation on HPT conditions, as the threshold has proven quantifiable with previous algorithms (Courtin et al., 2020; Prins, 2013). As secondary outcomes, we will explore PCI on TGI, whose threshold will be computed using a custom made algorithm, and we will complement perturbational complexity findings by calculating the avalanches-based functional repertoire.

Primary outcome: PCI indexes veridical  pain experience
If PCI reflects subjective experience, there may be differences in complexity between non-painful and painful HPT trials. Therefore, we expect that the difference between PCI values in painful vs non painful HPT trials will be significantly different from 0.

Secondary outcome: PCI indexes illusory pain experience
The same hypothesis holds for the TGI trials: the difference in PCI associated with painful vs non painful TGI trials should be significantly different from 0. 

Secondary outcome: functional repertoire
We expect that different conditions will exhibit different functional repertoires, as the number of functional states may change for networks processing different temperatures and painfulness levels. These differences may not match completely the differences in PCI, as the two complexity measures reflect different aspects of information processing.


3. Methods

Design
The study follows a sequential Bayes factor design[1.1] (Schönbrodt et al., 2017): a minimum number of Nmin= 20 participants will be enrolled in the study and the number of participants will be incremented in batches of 5 until the stopping conditions (defined below) are met or until we reach a maximum number of participants Nmax = 50. 
The primary outcome hypothesis will be used to guide the sequential sampling in this study. The evidence for a PCI difference between HPT painful and non painful trials will be quantified using a Bayesian paired two-tailed t-test (H1: presence of a difference, H0: absence of a difference). After the first data collection campaign (Nmin=20) and after each new batch acquisition (+5 participants) the Bayes Factor (BF₁₀) associated with the primary outcome hypothesis will be calculated. Evidence thresholds will be set according to current recommendations (Schönbrodt & Wagenmakers, 2018), where BF₁₀ ≥ 6 is taken as H1 stopping condition, and BF₁₀ ≤ 1/6 is considered the H0 stopping condition. Data collection will stop at the interim analysis if one of the two criteria is met, otherwise will proceed until completion.

Participants
Participants will take part in one experimental session that includes behavioral testing of thermal sensitivity and EEG recordings of neural complexity at Aarhus University Hospital. The study was approved by the Regional Ethics Committee. Participants will provide written informed consent and receive financial compensation. All volunteers will complete an eligibility interview before participating. Eligible participants will be healthy adults between 18 and 50 years of age who are fluent in English. Individuals will be excluded if they have current or past neurological, psychiatric, metabolic, or chronic pain conditions, if they take medications (except vitamin supplements and birth control pill), if they have used cannabis in the previous month, or alcohol in the previous 48h, if they have used other recreational drugs, or if they have dermatological issues, lesions, or scars on the volar forearm. Additional exclusion criteria include pregnancy or lactation, fewer than six hours of sleep before testing, current pain, metal implants, claustrophobia, or any condition likely to compromise EEG signal quality.

Stimuli
Thermal stimuli will be delivered using a Thermal Contact Stimulator II (TCSII; QST.Lab, France), mounted with a T11 probe. The stimulation surface comprises five independently controlled zones of 7.4 x 24.2 mm, corresponding to a total stimulation surface of 90 mm2. The maximal heating/cooling rate of this thermode is 100°C/s, and the baseline temperature will be 32°C. Across the behavioural and EEG sessions, the five zones will be set in fixed spatial patterns depending on the type of stimulus. For the thermal grill illusion (TGI), warm and cold zones alternate across the probe (warm–cold–warm–cold–warm). For cold-only stimulation, cold zones occupy the same positions (baseline–cold–baseline–cold–baseline), and the same structure applies for warm-only stimulation (warm–baseline–warm–baseline–warm). Each stimulus will ramp to its target temperature at the maximum heating or cooling rate and maintain that for 2 s. For TGI, the plateau begins when all areas reach their targets.

Procedure
The behavioural session will include thresholding and rating tasks designed to assess thermal grill illusion sensitivity and heat pain sensitivity on the volar surface of the dominant forearm. The behavioural session will last max 1 hour. All tasks are computer-based and programmed in MATLAB R2021a (The MathWorks, USA) using Psychtoolbox-3 ((Brainard, 1997; Kleiner 2007). [KM2.1]
The EEG session will involve a perceptual decision-making task, programmed in PsychoPy (Peirce et al., 2019), and will last up to 2 hours. 

Thresholding tasks
We will estimate both the TGI threshold and the heat pain threshold using adaptive psychophysical methods. In each trial, participants receive a thermal stimulus and report whether it feels painful (burning, pricking, aching, or similar). These binary responses will be used to fit psychometric functions that estimate the pain threshold (the temperature at which pain is reported on 50% of trials) and the slope (how quickly the probability of reporting pain changes around the threshold). Stimulus intensities will be selected adaptively using the Bayesian Ψ+ method (Kontsevich & Tyler, 1999; Prins, 2013), which tailors the sequence of stimuli to each participant’s sensitivity and efficiently concentrates sampling around their threshold.
For TGI and HPT thresholding, participants will complete an initial set of ten familiarization trials, followed by 80 experimental trials, arranged in four blocks. Each trial includes stimulus delivery (2 s at plateau), after which the participant will report whether they felt a painful or non-painful sensation using a binary keyboard response. The familiarisation trials will consist of temperatures between 10°C and 50°C, and will be excluded from analysis. The experimental trials use a two-dimensional extension of the Ψ method that adaptively selects warm–cold combinations to estimate the psychometric function relating heating and cooling levels to the probability of reporting pain. This allows us to derive the HPT threshold (when setting cooling = 0), the TGI threshold (the warm–cold combination producing pain on 50% of trials) and slope parameters (Appendix A). 

Temperature selection and threshold validation
Before the threshold validation blocks, participants will complete a short practice block to familiarize with the task. 
From the estimated heat pain psychometric function, we will derive three stimulus levels: a heat pain threshold (HPT) corresponding to a 50% probability of reporting a burning sensation, a suprathreshold heat pain (SHP) level associated with near-certain pain reports while remaining within safe temperature limits, and a non-noxious warm control associated with a near-zero probability of pain (warm-sub). When necessary, stimulus intensities will be constrained relative to the HPT to ensure appropriate spacing between conditions. These temperatures will be validated using a set of trials in which participants provide binary pain responses, with the goal of confirming that HPT elicits approximately half painful responses, SHP elicits consistently painful responses, and warm-sub yields to non-painful responses. If validation criteria are not met, temperatures will be modestly adjusted and re-tested within a limited number of iterations. Participants for whom a stable and interpretable calibration cannot be achieved will not proceed to the EEG session. 
The TGI thresholding procedures provide a range of warm–cold combinations capable of eliciting the illusion, but a single pair must be selected for the EEG session. The selected pair will be chosen to be as symmetrical as possible to the baseline and will be validated using a set of trials (TGI, warm-only, and cold-only conditions) in which participants provide binary pain responses. Validation will be considered successful if the TGI condition elicits a mix of painful and non-painful responses, while control conditions remain predominantly non-painful. If this pattern is not observed, several candidate temperature pairs will be tested iteratively until the validation criterion is met, within a limited number of attempts.

EEG task	
During the EEG session, participants will complete a perceptual decision-making task in which they receive thermal stimuli and indicate whether each one felt painful or non-painful. Each stimulus is delivered from a 32 °C baseline to the target temperature at a nominal rate of 10o °C/s, held for 2 s, and then returned to baseline. 
Participants then will answer a binary question (“burning/pricking/aching/painful?”) using the left (“Yes”) or right (“No”) arrow key, and subsequently provide a VAS rating of perceived intensity. Ratings are provided by moving a cursor along a scale ranging from “Not at all” (0) to “Extremely” (100), starting from the 0 position. Visual confirmation is shown after each keypress, and a “Too slow” message is displayed if no response is made within the allotted time (5 s for binary responses and 6 s for ratings). The interstimulus interval will be at least 15 s.
A total of 8 stimulation blocks of 30 stimuli will be delivered. Each stimulation block will be either a TGI block (A) or HPT block (B). Each participant will be randomly assigned to a block order (ABBA BAAB or BAAB ABBA), and a total of 30 stimuli each for warm, cold, warm-sub and SHP, and 60 stimuli for TGI and HPT temperatures will be employed.
To ensure balanced stimulus distribution throughout the session, trials will be pseudorandomized in blocks of four, each containing 1 cold, 1 warm, 2 TGI for TGI blocks, or 1 warm-sub, 1 SHP and 2 HPT for HPT blocks.
EEG acquisition
EEG data will be recorded using 64 active Ag/AgCl electrodes (Brain Products actiCAP) mounted in an elastic cap and arranged according to the international 10–10 system. Electrode impedance will be adjusted to ensure sufficient signal quality (<25 kΩ). The signal will be digitized at 1000 Hz and referenced online to the average of all electrodes. 

EEG Preprocessing
EEG recordings will be pre-processed in Python (version 3.12.3) using the mne (Gramfort et al., 2013; Larson et al., 2024) and mne_bids (Appelhoff et al., 2019) packages. Each recording will be visually inspected to identify flat or excessively noisy channels, which will be interpolated using spline interpolation. Participants with more than 30% of bad channels will be excluded. Data will be re-referenced to the average, and independent component analysis (ICA) will be applied to identify and remove components reflecting stereotyped artifacts (e.g., ocular, muscular, cardiac, or channel-specific noise). Continuous data will then be notch-filtered at 50Hz and bandpass-filtered (0.1-45Hz, second-order Butterworth). The signal will be segmented into epochs from –700 ms to 2500 ms relative to stimulus onset and baseline-corrected using the –500 to –10 ms window. Epochs with peak-to-peak amplitudes exceeding 200 μV will be rejected. A trial will be considered valid only if it includes a binary pain/no-pain response and an artefact-free EEG trace after preprocessing. Any condition with fewer than 17 valid epochs will be excluded. 

PCI calculation
PCI will be calculated for each condition following the state transition-based approach developed by Comolatti et al. (Comolatti et al., 2019). First, singular value decomposition will be applied to the signal matrix, and the components whose cumulative energy accounts for at least 99% of the response will be retained (a proxy for spatial differentiation). Among these, only components with a signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) of at least 1.1 will be retained for further analysis. This minimum SNR value is consistent with our previous work using thermal stimuli (Montemagno et al., 2025). If no component exceeds the SNR threshold, the algorithm will assign a PCI value of zero. PCI is then estimated in terms of temporal differentiation by computing the number of state transitions (NST) derived from recurrence quantification analysis. For each retained component, distance matrices of voltage-amplitude differences across time will be computed separately for pre-stimulus and post-stimulus periods. The pre-stimulus window will match previously established values (−0.50 s to −0.01 s). To evaluate the post-stimulus period, PCI will be calculated using a 1 s window. For each component, the distance matrices will be thresholded at multiple levels (𝜀n) to generate binary transition matrices marking the time points at which the signal crosses each threshold, quantifying transitions between distinct states. Because NST is evaluated across several thresholds, the threshold 𝜀n* that maximizes the difference in NST between the post- and pre-stimulus windows (ΔNST) will be identified for each component. The ΔNST values will then be summed across components to obtain the PCI for that condition. The parameter k, which controls the relative contribution of pre- and post-stimulus NST during thresholding, will be fixed at 1.1 in accordance with the original implementation (Comolatti et al., 2019).  
To ensure comparability across conditions, PCI will be computed from ERPs averaged over the same number of epochs per condition, corresponding to the maximum number of valid trials available across all conditions and participants (n). Conditions with fewer than 17 valid epochs will be excluded, as ERPs derived from fewer trials are probably unreliable and PCI is sensitive to signal-to-noise ratio. 
In order to mitigate the effect of single epochs over PCI values, a bootstrapping approach will be used: for each condition, the first 17 epochs will be used as a pool from which to draw 15 random epochs without replacement, in order to obtain a total of 500 draws. The mean of the PCI distribution across draws will be used to approximate the real PCI value associated with the condition.
For HPT and TGIT conditions, PCI will be calculated separately on the entire conditions, and after splitting and averaging the epochs in painful (p) and non painful (np) trials so to obtain 4 others subconditions (TGIT-p, TGIT-np, HPT-p, HPT-np).

Functional repertoire calculation
We will estimate the functional repertoire using the algorithm developed by Breyton et al. (Breyton et al., 2025). This method estimates neural avalanches, short-lived patterns of electrode activation, by z-scoring and thresholding the EEG signal (threshold z = 3 SD) to binarize the time series. Although avalanche analysis is typically applied to continuous resting-state data, evidence suggests that stimulus-evoked activity exhibits similar underlying dynamics (Arviv et al., 2019). Further, because avalanches are brief events occurring at a time scale shorter than our epoch duration, it is theoretically possible to safely concatenate epochs instead of using directly raw data: nonetheless, to ensure that no spurious avalanche may be detected across two epochs, we will zero pad the beginning and the end of each epoch after z scoring. 
For each condition, we will randomly select n epochs (where n matches the number used for PCI computation) and z-score the signal across all conditions in each block (warm, cold, TGI for TGI blocks; warm-sub, SHP, HPT for HPT blocks): this guarantees the comparability between TGI (or HPT) stimuli and their controls, that would not be possible if each condition was z-scored individually. For the sub-conditions TGI-p, TGI-np, HPT-p, HPT-np subconditions the z scoring will be conducted on the entire condition (TGI or HPT, respectively), as we are interested in the within-condition difference (e.g. painful vs non painful inside HPT).
 From each epoch, we will extract two time windows: baseline (-1 to 0 s) and response (0 to 1 s). Within each window, the time series will be concatenated across epochs and avalanches will be identified. The functional repertoire will then be computed as the number of distinct spatial patterns occurring over time (Breyton et al., 2025). The process will be repeated for 500 iterations and the mean of the draws distribution will be used to approximate one value of functional repertoire for each time window. Finally, because we analyse stimulus-evoked rather than resting-state activity, we will compute the response-to-baseline functional repertoire ratio to quantify changes in brain dynamics following stimulation.


4. Statistical analysis
4.1. Primary outcome: does PCI reflect subjective pain experience in HPT?
At each look, we will perform a two-tailed paired Bayesian t-test to assess the evidence supporting either the hypothesis (H1) that the mean difference between painful and non painful HPT trials is different from zero, or the null hypothesis (H0). Bayes Factors (BF10) will be calculated using the package BayesFactor for the software R, with default priors (Morey & Rouder, 2012). Compatibly with recommendations by Schönbrodt et al (Schönbrodt et al., 2017; Schönbrodt & Wagenmakers, 2018) BF10 > 6 and BF10 < 1/6 will be used as stopping criteria respectively.

4.2 Secondary outcomes: testing subjective and objective stimulus features
At the conclusion of the data acquisition (either due to early stopping or having reached the maximum number of participants) secondary outcomes analyses will be performed. 

4.2.1 Do PCI index pain experience in TGI?
Similarly to the HPT condition, we will perform a two-tailed paired Bayesian t-test to establish the strength of the evidence supporting a mean difference of PCI in painful and non painful TGI condition (H1) or the absence of difference. We will use the same Jeffreys prior employed in the HPT Bayesian t-test. 
For this test and for the following analyses, thresholds for qualifying the strength of evidence using Bayes Factors will follow common interpretative conventions: BF10 < 0.032 very strong evidence for H0,  BF10 < 0.1 strong evidence for H0, BF10 <0.32 moderate evidence for H0, BF10 < 1 anecdotal evidence for H0, BF10 > 1 anecdotal evidence for H1, BF10 > 3.2 moderate evidence for H1, BF10 > 10 strong evidence for H1, BF10 > 32 very strong evidence for H1.

4.2.2 Do PCI capture objective stimulus features?
In order to test for the temperature effect on complexity we will fit the following model:

PCI ~ Type + 1|Participant

where PCI corresponds to the raw PCI values (but may undergo a log or a square-root transformation to ensure normality of the residuals). Type is a categorical variable referring to stimulus type (warm, cold, SHP, TGI, HPT, warm-sub), and Participant is a categorical variable indexing participant number. 
Since warm and cold stimuli are supposed to be largely non painful, but some exceptions could occur, only the trials with a 0 response will be used for ERP averaging and PCI calculation; conversely, since heat stimuli are supposed to be largely painful, only trials with a 1 response will be used for analysis.	 
	A Bayesian ANOVA will be used to investigate the strength of the evidence supporting the presence of PCI differences across different groups using the function anovaBF for R software with default priors. If the ANOVA yields a BF10 > 3.2 supporting the inclusion of the Type variable, post hoc comparisons using Bayesian t-tests will be conducted to understand which conditions drive the difference.

4.2.3 Does functional repertoire capture subjective or objective stimulus features?

Functional repertoire will be explored in the same way, both by performing two-tailed paired Bayesian t-tests to assess the strength of evidence in favour of a HPT-p vs HPT-np and TGI-p vs TGI-np mean difference, and by using a Bayesian ANOVA to investigate objective stimulus differences after fitting the following model:	

Functional_repertoire ~ Type + 1|Participant

In this case, though, since we cannot compare across TGI and HPT blocks because of the z-scoring choice, the model will be applied separately to TGI and HPT blocks (e.g. Type = cold, warm or TGI for one model and Type = warm-sub, SHP, HPT for the other).

	4.2.4 Are PCI and functional repertoire correlated?
To investigate whether PCI and functional repertoire capture different aspects of the stimuli, we will calculate the Bayes factor of their correlation using the function correlationBF in R.

Appendix A. Threshold estimation

The TGI threshold is estimated using a two-dimensional Bayesian Ψ method that adaptively selects warm–cold combinations to estimate the psychometric surface relating heating intensity h and cooling intensity c to the probability of reporting pain. The surface function is defined by the following three expressions:
threshold function:  α_c=〖   α〗_10+(α_10-α_32 )⋅(1-c/(32-10))^(κ_α )  
slope function: β_c=exp(〖log(β〗_10)+(log(β_10)-〖log(β〗_32))  〖⋅(1-c/(32-10))〗^(κ_β ) )  
marginal PF: P(response=pain|h,c)=γ + (1-γ-λ) ⋅Φ(β_c (h-α_c ))  
where:
Φ = standard Gaussian cumulative distribution function (CDF)
α(c) = heat pain threshold as a function of cooling
β(c) = slope as a function of cooling
γ = guess rate (false-positive pain reports)
λ = lapse rate (false-negative pain reports)
The TGI psychometric surface is defined by eight parameters. Guess and lapse rates are fixed at 2%, and the remaining six parameters are estimated using a Bayesian Ψ method. Because the Palamedes toolbox does not support three-dimensional psychometric functions, we implemented a custom extension based on Palamedes. The algorithm maintains an approximate joint posterior distribution over all parameters and updates it after each response of the participant. The parameter space and grid ranges were informed by a previous TGI thresholding dataset (Deolindo et al., 2025). Heating intensities can vary from 32°C to 50°C in 1°C steps, and cooling intensities from 10°C to 32°C in 2°C steps. The Ψ method selects from these options on each trial to maximize expected information gain about the psychometric surface. The grid for the joint posterior distribution includes the parameter ranges reported in Table A1.

 
Table A1
	Parameter	Description	Grid values
1	α10	Threshold when cooling is at 10°C	7 values from −40°C to 32°C
2	α32	Threshold when cooling is at 32°C	7 values from 6°C to 22°C
3	κα
Curvature of the threshold function across cooling	7 values from −3 to 1
4	log(β10)
Log slope when cooling is at 10°C	7 values from −4 to 0
5	log(β32)
Log slope when cooling is at 32°C	7 values from −2 to 2
6	κβ
Curvature of the slope function across cooling	7 values from −2 to 2
7	γ	Guess rate	Fixed at 2%
8	λ	Lapse rate	Fixed at 2%
 
Appendix B. Sample size justification - Sequential Bayes Factor design
For this experiment, we use a sequential Bayes factor design with a fixed maximum number of participants because of resource constraint (n_max = 50). 
Given the novelty of applying complexity analysis to EEG signals elicited by thermosensory stimuli, we are unsure about the effect size we can expect. However, our previous study showed Cohen’s d values ranging from 0.3 to 1.4 for PCI differences caused by stimulus temperature manipulation. Based on this, we decided to set our lowest effect size of interest to Cohen’s d=0.5. Since the effect size might be larger, we decided to use a sequential Bayes factor design which will allow us to terminate data collection early if the effect size is big enough. 
In order to calculate the sample size for this experiment we used a simulation approach based on (Schönbrodt et al., 2017; Schönbrodt & Wagenmakers, 2018) and implemented in R using the BayesFactor package. The simulation code has been developed by Alex Quent and can be found in the github repository:	 https://github.com/MRC-CBU/cbu_bayesian_sequential_designs/tree/main?tab=readme-ov-file[3.1].
We conducted simulations to determine the probability of our study to conclusively select H0 or H1 as well as the probability of incorrectly selecting one hypothesis over the other. The hypothetical data distribution (standardized mean difference of HPT painful - non painful trials) was modeled as a normal distribution with a mean of either 0 or 0.5 and unitary standard deviation, corresponding to true latent standardized effect sizes of Cohen’s d 0 or 0.5. We then calculated the Bayes Factor associated with the paired two-tailed t-test for the first random Nmin = 20 participants drawn from this distribution using the default prior of the function ttestBF for the effect size distribution. If the BF10 was comprised between 1/6 and 6 (stopping condition for H0 and H1, respectively), we incremented the number of participants by Nbatch = 5, and repeated the process until the BF10 met the stopping conditions or until the maximum number of participants (nmax=50 in the simulation) was reached. We repeated this process for 10’000 iterations under each hypothesis (H1: d = 0.5 ; H0: d = 0). At the end of each simulation we computed the percentage of the simulations concluded because the stopping conditions for H0, H1 were met, or concluded because nmax  participants have been recruited.
The percentage of simulations concluded because they reached the maximum number of participants is 20.5%, meaning that in 79.5% of the cases we will conclude the study with a fewer number of subjects.
Following common interpretative conventions, in which BF > 3.2 represents moderate evidence in favour of H1, and BF < 0.32 moderate evidence in favour of H0, we estimated our risk of mistakenly supporting H1 in absence of a real effect to be 2.2%, while 87.8% correctly support H0 and 10.0% prove inconclusive. Conversely, our risk of mistakenly supporting H0 in presence of a real effect is 4.1%, while 80.9% of the stimulations correctly support H1 and 15.0% prove inconclusive.
By setting  a nmin= 20 and nmax = 50 subjects we will have an acceptable risk of mistakenly being conclusive for the incorrect hypothesis. In parallel, we will have more than 80% probability of the study selecting a conclusive hypothesis.[4.1]

 
Fig 1. Stopping criteria under H1
Plot showing the percentage of simulations for each outcome (stopping for H0 criterion in red, stopping for H1 in greed, stopping for reaching the maximum number of participants in blue) for each maximum number of participants enrolled in the studio. The simulated population had an effect size d = 0.5 (H1 hypothesis) and the stopping conditions were set to BF10 <1/6 for H0 and BF10 > 6 for H1. Note that for 50 maximum participants, slightly more than 20% of the simulations stops because it reaches the maximum number of participants.

 
Fig 2. Stopping criteria under H0
Plot showing the percentage of simulations for each outcome (stopping for H0 in red, stopping for H1 in green, stopping for reaching the maximum number of participants in blue) for each maximum number of participants enrolled in the studio. The simulated population had an effect size d = 0 (H0 hypothesis) and the stopping conditions were set to BF10 <⅙ for H0 and BF10 > 6 for H1. Note that for 50 maximum participants, slightly less than 80% of the simulations ends up supporting H0, and less than 5% of the simulations supports H1 (false positive results).

title: NeurosynthMaps
Anxiety Frontal Lobe
Response to Ruby et al: On a ‘failed’ attempt to manipulate conscious perception with transcranial magnetic stimulation to prefrontal cortex
description: Does disruption of prefrontal cortical activity using transcranial magnetic stimulation (TMS) impair visual metacognition? An initial study supporting this idea (Rounis, Maniscalco, Rothwell, Passingham, & Lau, 2010) motivated an attempted replication and extension (Bor, Schwartzman, Barrett, & Seth, 2017). Bor et al. failed to replicate the initial study, concluding that there was not good evidence that TMS to prefrontal cortex impairs visual metacognition. This failed replication has recently been critiqued by some of the authors of the initial study (Ruby, Maniscalco, Lau, & Peters, 2017). Here, we argue that these recent criticisms are misplaced. In responding to the criticisms of Ruby et al, we encounter some more general issues concerning good practice in replication of cognitive neuroscience studies, and in setting criteria for excluding data when employing statistical analyses like signal detection theory. We look forward to further studies of the role of prefrontal cortex in metacognition, with increasingly refined methodologies, motivated by the discussions in this series of papers.

title: Ventrolateral Prefrontal Cortex Updates Chosen Value According to Choice Set Size
Insight and non-insight problem solving: A heart rate variability study
description: Occasionally, problems are solved with a sudden Aha! Moment (insight), while the mundane approach to solve problems is analytical (non-insight). At first glance, non-insight appears to depend on the availability and taxation of cognitive resources to execute the step-by-step approach, whereas insight does not or to a lesser extent. However, this remains debated. To investigate the reliance of both solution types on cognitive resources, we assessed the involvement of the prefrontal cortex using vagally mediated heart rate variability (vmHRV) as an index. Participants (N = 68) solved 70 compound remote associates word puzzles solvable with insight and non-insight. Before, during, and after solving the word puzzles, we measured vmHRV. Our results showed that resting-state vmHRV (trait) showed a negative association with behavioural performance for both solution types. This might reflect inter-individual differences in inhibitory control. As the solution search requires one to think of remote associations, inhibitory control might hamper rather than aid this process. Furthermore, we observed, for both solution types, a vmHRV increase from resting-state to solution search (state), lingering on in the post-task recovery period. This could mark the increase of prefrontal resources to promote an open-minded stance, essential for divergent thinking, which arguably is crucial for this task. Our findings suggest that, at a general level, both solution types share common aspects. However, a closer analysis of early and late solutions and puzzle difficulty suggested that metacognitive differentiation between insight and non-insight improved with higher trait vmHRV, and that a unique association between trait vmHRV and puzzle difficulty was present for each solution type.
